title: Consistent Hashing and Random Trees: Distributed Caching Protocols for Relieving Hot Spots on the World Wide Web
author: David Karger, Eric Lehman, Tom Leighton, Matthew Levine, Daniel Lewin, Rina Panigrahy (STOC 1997)
note: The original paper that introduced consistent hashing, proving that adding or removing a node remaps only a small fraction of keys -- the foundation of hash-based rebalancing in Dynamo, Cassandra, and many caches.

title: Herding elephants: Lessons learned from sharding Postgres at Notion
author: Garrett Fidalgo (Notion Engineering)
note: Case study of sharding by workspace ID (multitenancy) into 480 logical shards on 32 physical databases, chosen because 480 has many divisors for future rebalancing; covers double-writes and verification during migration.

title: How Figma's databases team lived to tell the scale
author: Sammy Steele (Figma Engineering)
note: Nine-month horizontal sharding of Postgres via a custom DBProxy query router and 'colos' of tables sharing a shard key, separating logical from physical sharding to cut over with about ten seconds of unavailability.

title: How Discord Stores Trillions of Messages
author: Bo Ingram (Discord Engineering)
note: Shows hot-partition pain in practice: huge servers create skewed partitions in Cassandra, which Discord relieved with request coalescing and consistent-hash-based routing during its migration to ScyllaDB.

title: Vitess Reference: Sharding
author: Vitess project (CNCF)
note: Explains key ranges, Vindexes as pluggable sharding functions, and live resharding where new shards are copied and kept up to date while old shards keep serving traffic -- a concrete model for operational rebalancing.

title: MongoDB Manual: Sharding
author: MongoDB, Inc.
note: Production docs contrasting ranged vs hashed sharding trade-offs (targeted range queries vs even distribution under monotonic keys), plus mongos request routing and the automatic balancer.

title: Best practices for designing and using partition keys effectively in DynamoDB
author: Amazon Web Services
note: Quantifies hot-spot limits (3,000 RCU / 1,000 WCU per partition) and prescribes high-cardinality keys, write sharding with random suffixes, and adaptive capacity -- directly mirrors the chapter's skewed-workload section.

title: CMU 15-445/645 Lecture 23: Introduction to Distributed Databases (Fall 2025)
author: Andy Pavlo (Carnegie Mellon University)
note: University lecture notes covering horizontal partitioning schemes (hash, range, consistent hashing) and shared-nothing architectures, giving an academic framing that complements the chapter's industrial examples.
